narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application like they're piecing together the story of your journey to becoming a lawyer, drawing from patterns they've seen in thousands of students before you. Your story starts strong in their eyes with your first-year grades landing you in the bottom decile of your school—that was the biggest signal to them, whispering concerns about early struggles that often foreshadow bigger hurdles ahead, like the bar exam. It didn't get much better by your third year, where you stayed in that same bottom decile, reinforcing their worry that academic momentum might be hard to build. Even with your family's high income, which usually opens doors, it somehow painted a picture here of potential distractions or mismatched priorities that have tripped up others in similar spots. Your LSAT score of 38, while respectable, added to the caution, suggesting the intense analytical demands of law school and the bar might stretch you thin. As a white male going part-time, those details layered on subtle risks they've seen play out before, from divided focus with work to demographic trends in who pushes through. Your undergrad GPA of 2.8 fit right into this narrative, hinting at a consistent pattern rather than a breakout moment. Together, these elements tipped the scales to a 44.6% estimated chance of not passing the bar, leading to the tough call to reject your application—it's not about your potential as a person, but protecting the program's success stories. The good news? This is just one snapshot; with targeted prep on those early-grade building blocks, you could rewrite your story elsewhere. You've got the drive—high family support is a real asset—keep pushing. What small step, like extra practice tests, could shift your trajectory next time? We're rooting for you to prove them wrong.
